title: Affinity Diagrams — Bottom-Up Qualitative Synthesis
content: An affinity diagram organises qualitative research data into themed clusters using bottom-up synthesis. Process: 1) Extract individual data points (observations, quotes, notes) from raw research. 2) Group related points into natural clusters — do NOT start with predefined categories. 3) Name each cluster with a descriptive theme label that captures its essence. 4) Organise clusters into a hierarchy of 3–5 top-level themes. 5) Write insight statements for each theme that capture the 'so what?' 6) Note frequency, intensity, and connections between themes. 7) Prioritise insights by impact on design decisions. Best for: synthesising interviews, observations, surveys, and any large body of qualitative data. The key discipline is bottom-up — let themes emerge from the data, never impose structure first.

title: Card Sort Analysis — Informing Information Architecture
content: Card sorting reveals how users mentally group and label information. Two types: Open sort (users create their own categories — reveals mental models) and Closed sort (users sort into predefined categories — validates proposed IA). Analysis: 1) Identify common category patterns and naming conventions across participants. 2) Build a similarity matrix showing how frequently items were grouped together. 3) Flag ambiguous items that were inconsistently categorised. 4) Recommend navigation structure based on user mental models, not organisational structure. Ideal sample: 15–30 participants for quantitative patterns, 5–8 for qualitative insights. Follow-up with tree testing to validate the proposed IA. Common mistake: designing navigation around business org chart instead of user mental models.

title: Diary Studies — Longitudinal Behaviour in Context
content: Diary studies capture user behaviour over time in natural contexts — revealing patterns that single-session research misses. Design: 1) Duration: typically 1–4 weeks depending on behaviour frequency. 2) Participants: 8–15 people with screening criteria and compensation plan. 3) Entry prompts: mix of daily scheduled prompts and event-triggered prompts ('When you just used X, describe...'). 4) Capture methods: photo, video, text, or voice — match to participant convenience. 5) Check-ins: mid-study interviews or pulse surveys to maintain engagement. 6) Onboarding: briefing session, practice entries, tool setup. Analysis: code and theme entries across participants, looking for temporal patterns. Plan for 20–30% attrition — over-recruit and use engagement strategies (reminders, incentives per entry). Best for: understanding habits, routines, context switching, and behaviours that change over time.
source: Flaherty — Diary Studies: Understanding Long-Term User Behavior

title: Empathy Maps — Externalising User Understanding
content: An empathy map (Dave Gray, XPLANE) is a collaborative tool to externalise what a team knows about a user type across four quadrants: Says (direct quotes and statements from research), Thinks (inferred beliefs, concerns, and internal thoughts), Does (observable actions, behaviours, and workarounds), Feels (emotional states, anxieties, and motivations). Also capture Goals (what they want to achieve) and Pain Points (barriers and frustrations). Critical rule: build from actual research data, not assumptions — if a quadrant is empty, that's a research gap, not a creative writing prompt. Best created as a team exercise after interviews or observation. The map should feel like a real person the team can empathise with. Output: design implications and identified research gaps that need filling.
source: Dave Gray — Gamestorming; Gothelf & Seiden — Lean UX

title: User Interview Scripts — Structured Qualitative Conversations
content: A user interview script ensures consistent, insightful qualitative research. Structure: Introduction (2–3 min) — welcome, purpose, consent. Warm-up (3–5 min) — easy context questions about background and role. Core exploration (20–30 min) — deep questions organised by research theme with follow-up probes. Specific scenarios (10–15 min) — walk-through of actual tasks or experiences. Wrap-up (3–5 min) — summary, 'anything we missed?', next steps. Key techniques: funnel approach (broad context → specific features), JTBD probing ('When did you last...? What were you trying to accomplish?'), and follow-up probes ('Tell me more', 'Why was that important?', 'What happened next?'). Rules: avoid leading questions, avoid hypotheticals, avoid yes/no questions. Stay neutral — your job is to understand, not validate.
source: Steve Portigal — Interviewing Users; Erika Hall — Just Enough Research

title: Jobs-to-Be-Done — Motivation-Centred Design
content: JTBD framework (Clayton Christensen, Tony Ulwick): people hire products to get a job done — focus on the job, not the product. Three dimensions: Functional (the practical task or outcome), Emotional (how they want to feel), Social (how they want to be perceived). Job statement format: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome].' Map the full job lifecycle: define → locate → prepare → confirm → execute → monitor → modify → conclude. For each stage, identify outcome expectations — what does success look like? Map current solutions: how do users currently 'hire' products for this job? Find opportunities where current solutions underserve the job. JTBD reframes product decisions around user motivations rather than features, preventing the trap of building what competitors build instead of what users need.
source: Clayton Christensen — Competing Against Luck; Tony Ulwick — Outcome-Driven Innovation

title: Journey Maps — End-to-End Experience Visualisation
content: A journey map visualises the end-to-end experience across stages, touchpoints, channels, and emotions. Process: 1) Define scope — persona, scenario, start and end points. 2) Identify 5–7 journey stages from awareness through post-use/advocacy. 3) Map each stage: user goals, actions, touchpoints and channels, thoughts and questions, emotional state (positive/negative scale), pain points and friction, opportunity areas. 4) Visualise the emotional curve showing how feelings rise and fall. 5) Prioritise top 3–5 design opportunities by impact and feasibility. 6) Identify moments of truth — critical moments that make or break the experience. Journey maps should be persona-specific and grounded in research data. Map current state (as-is) before designing future state (to-be). Include both digital and physical touchpoints.
source: Jim Kalbach — Mapping Experiences; Jesse James Garrett — The Elements of User Experience

title: Interview Synthesis — From Transcript to Actionable Insights
content: Structured interview synthesis transforms raw transcripts into shareable findings. Framework: 1) Participant profile — role, context, experience level. 2) Key themes — 3–5 major themes with supporting quotes. 3) Jobs-to-be-done — what the participant is trying to accomplish. 4) Pain points — frustrations, barriers, unmet needs (with severity rating). 5) Workarounds — how they currently solve problems (reveals design opportunities). 6) Delighters — what works well or exceeds expectations. 7) Notable quotes — 5–8 verbatim quotes capturing key insights. 8) Surprises — anything unexpected or counter to assumptions. 9) Action items — specific design or research follow-ups. For each insight, rate confidence: explicitly stated vs inferred. Present in a scannable format suitable for stakeholders who won't read the full transcript.

title: Usability Test Plans — Measuring Design Effectiveness
content: A usability test plan structures evaluation of designs and prototypes. Components: 1) Research questions — 3–5 specific questions to answer. 2) Methodology — moderated vs unmoderated, remote vs in-person, think-aloud protocol. 3) Participants — screening criteria, 5–8 per segment, recruitment approach. 4) Tasks — 5–8 realistic scenarios with success criteria and expected completion time. 5) Metrics — task success rate, time on task, error rate, SUS/SEQ scores, satisfaction rating. 6) Facilitation guide — script for introduction, task delivery, probing, and debrief. 7) Data collection — what to record, observation template, note-taking framework. 8) Analysis plan — how findings will be synthesised and prioritised. Always run a pilot test before real sessions. Five users per segment finds approximately 80% of usability issues (Nielsen). Moderated testing reveals the 'why' behind behaviour; unmoderated testing scales for quantitative confidence.
source: Steve Krug — Rocket Surgery Made Easy; Tullis & Albert — Measuring the User Experience

title: User Personas — Research-Grounded Archetypes
content: Personas (Alan Cooper) are archetypical users based on behavioural patterns, not demographics alone. Each persona should feel like a real person the team can empathise with. Creation: 1) Analyse research data to find clusters of behaviours, motivations, and needs. 2) Define 2–4 personas, each with: name and one-line quote capturing their mindset, demographics (age range, occupation, tech comfort), goals (functional, emotional, social), frustrations (current pain points), behaviours (how they currently approach the problem), a day-in-the-life scenario, and design implications. 3) Identify the primary persona — the one the design must satisfy first. 4) Highlight research gaps that would strengthen the personas. Critical rule: personas must be grounded in actual research data, not assumptions. Assumption-based personas are worse than no personas — they give false confidence. Update personas as new research emerges.
source: Alan Cooper — About Face; Gothelf & Seiden — Lean UX; Erika Hall — Just Enough Research
